data.title: The Market Can Still Climb This Wall Of Worry - But Not Yet
data.description:
Trying to predict President Trump's next step in the war and trade based on that makes no sense to me.

That said, the recent headlines have not been particularly encouraging for the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Some sources suggest that Trump may be willing to end the war in the near term, without reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

At the same time, Trump made the following remark (addressed to the U.S. allies, particularly in Europe) in one of his Truth Social posts, which is anything but reassuring from a Strait of Hormuz reopening perspective:

You’ll have to start learning how to fight for yourself, the U.S.A. won’t be there to help you anymore, just like you weren’t there for us. Iran has been, essentially, decimated. The hard part is done. Go get your own oil!

It's not that I disagree with his remarks. What makes me concerned is the market reaction to that post. Here is the market tape for March 31 (pre-market, which is when I'm writing this piece):

Pretty much everything is in the green, across all sectors and all U.S. benchmarks. The VIX is down, and so are the Brent and WTI futures.

Call me crazy on this one, but given that there is no progress on reopening the Strait of Hormuz (which is the main pressure on the U.S. equity markets since the start of the war), this feels like a dead cat bounce.

Needless to say, I'm not buying the dip yet, and I reiterate my 6,150 target for the S&P 500 in H1 this year.

In this piece, I will zoom out and look beyond the war headlines. I will discuss my view on what I consider one of the largest bricks in the wall of worry that the market will have to climb in the second half of the year.

My argument here is that the market will indeed climb this wall of worry in the back half of the year. That's why I believe we are in a correction, and not in a bear market.

Let me start with that brick in the wall of worry.

How Durable Is the U.S. Equity Earnings Story?

Let's leave the elephant in the room (i.e., the oil shock due to the Hormuz blockade) for later.

So far, the S&P 500 has been able to absorb high interest rates, as economic activity in the U.S. was strong enough to protect earnings growth. In fact, as seen below, since mid-2025, the expectations for forward EPS growth have increased exponentially among S&P 500 constituents.

Therefore, recent developments like the one below make me increasingly concerned about the continuation of the current strong growth cycle.

The chart above is from the BEA's March 13 update, when real GDP growth in Q4 2025 was revised to 0.7% (annualized), down sharply from 4.4% in Q3.

Based on the BEA's second estimate, it appears that real final sales to private domestic purchasers rose only 1.9% in Q4 vs 2.4% in the advance estimate.

Out of all the line items that I monitor in the GDP estimate, final sales to private domestic purchasers are one of my go-to items for gauging the state of private sector demand.

Therefore, the chart above suggests that underlying domestic demand had already cooled before the oil shock hit in March. On top of that, January's real PCE increased by only 0.1% month over month. As a side note, the February 2026 PCE release is only scheduled for April 9, 2026. Why is that a problem?

Well, as seen below, the real PCE MoM change has slowed down since September last year and is now barely above 0%.

Again, based on the first chart in this section, it seems that the Street is quite bullish on continued demand resilience translating into earnings growth. The data I presented so far, however, suggests a slowdown in consumer spending prior (emphasis) to the oil shock.

Looking at unemployment now, February's nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 (after a January increase of 126,000), and the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%.

To be crystal clear, I am not worried about the unemployment rate right now in the U.S. In fact, one of my favorite indicators of a recession is the Sahm Rule Recession Indicator, which looks at the average U.S. unemployment rate over the last three months and compares it with the lowest three-month average from the past year.

For readers new to this metric, a simple way to interpret the figure is with this example: a reading of 0.27 (which is the latest value of this indicator) means the current three-month average unemployment rate is 0.27 percentage points above its lowest three-month average from the last 12 months.

A reading of 0.50 or higher is the threshold that signals recession risk under the Sahm Rule. Funny enough, the indicator in July, August, and September 2024 came in slightly above 0.5. That's when I started to (naively) think that AI adoption was behind the hike in unemployment.

It appears that I was dead wrong. There are many headlines (like the one below) that suggest that the hike in tech layoffs may be due to AI spending, not AI adoption.

Here is a very interesting remark from that article:

Promises of a leaner approach have been welcomed on Wall Street, especially from tech giants that are trying to justify huge amounts of capital spending to pay for the data centers, chips and other components required to power AI technology.

Back to unemployment, what concerns me is the trend, and the fact that the AI adoption may not really be the driver behind the higher unemployment in the last two years, versus the start of the AI supercycle back in early 2023 (or late 2022, if you were really switched on with the AI industry).

Overall, outside of a few companies that are clearly benefiting from AI adoption, like Palantir or the big two LLM startups (OpenAI and Anthropic), I remain skeptical of a near-term broad adoption of AI agents, particularly in the enterprise segment.

To be clear, this may happen in 3-5 years, but it ain't happening today. This means that AI-driven earnings growth remains limited for the majority of companies if the macro backdrop continues to deteriorate this year.

Why I'm Not A Bear

I strongly believe that by the second half of the year, investors will have largely forgotten about this growth concern if the following two conditions are both satisfied.

The Strait Of Hormuz "Blockade"

First, the Strait of Hormuz needs to reopen (fast). Headlines like the one below make me increasingly concerned that a quick reopening of the strait is unlikely in the next 1-2 weeks.

Yes, yes, yes. I know. There is often a gap between what Trump says and what he does.

However, the conflict in the Middle East is far from de-escalating, especially after Iran's latest attack on an oil tanker off the coast of Dubai, following Trump's recent warnings that the U.S. will obliterate the country's main energy plants if it does not agree to a peace deal and open the Strait of Hormuz.

If I look at the data, tanker transits are far below the historical average, despite the fact that Trump said Iran agreed to allow 20 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz starting this Monday.

That said, just like last year, I believe this exogenous event may come to a quick end, as it's not in the interest of any of the parties involved to keep the Strait closed for an extended period of time.

If I had to make my own call, I would say the Strait of Hormuz reopens in 2-3 weeks, with positive talks between both parties before the April 6 deadline.

If you think I may be too optimistic, bear in mind that some analysts are projecting a normalization by early April:

Notwithstanding uncertainty about the ceasefire negotiations, in our base case, ‌we ⁠expect traffic through the Strait to normalize by early April, which would be consistent with Brent averaging $85/b in 2026.- Barclays

Once this overhang is removed from investors' minds, I foresee another bull run, similar to what happened last year. The continuation of which will largely depend on the second factor.

Warsh Needs to Aggressively Cut Rates

Despite the data that I presented in the last section, in its last SEP, the Fed increased its economic growth forecast for 2026 to 2.4% vs 2.3% in December. On top of that, they left the unemployment rate unchanged to 4.4%, forecasting a decrease to 4.3% in 2027.

The bears are arguing that the market is no longer projecting rate cuts this year, with some estimates suggesting nearly a 30% chance of a hike this year.

I think these estimates are dead wrong. There are two reasons why.

First, the Fed tends to look through oil shocks, as they tend to be transitory in nature and have a limited impact on core inflation (which excludes food and energy). Here is a remark from Chairman Powell during the last FOMC meeting:

So the first thing is, there is the original thinking—the long-standing thinking, put it that way, is that you do “look through” energy shocks...

In fact, during his appearance this week at Harvard University, Powell leaned again on a wait-and-see approach amid this geopolitical uncertainty:

We ‌feel like our policy's in a good place for us to wait and see how that turns out

My second argument is that, despite the headlines, the Fed is still looking at a rate cut this year.

In the latest dot plot chart, the expectation was still largely for one rate cut in 2026, with little dispersion across FOMC participants:

Bottom line, I think Kevin Warsh still has a case to cut interest rates this year as once his nomination goes through and he takes over from Powell in May this year. By June, I expect the oil shock to fade, with Brent likely in the mid $70s and the S&P 500 on track to retest new highs.

As I explained in many of my macro articles, I think Warsh will be dovish. My rationale is (annoyingly) simple. Here is an excerpt from one of my articles:

I’m not worried in the slightest about rate cuts in the second half of the year. In fact, I wouldn't completely rule out a jumbo cut (that is, 50 bps) in the June meeting, likely driven by Trump’s pressure on Warsh to lower interest rates.

In my view, the lowest hanging fruit for the Republicans to increase their chances of winning the mid-term elections is tackling the affordability problem in the U.S., starting with lower mortgage rates (which, by the way, have increased 40 bps since the war started):

Prediction markets suggest that odds for the mid-term elections are in favor of the Democratic Party, which means that the Trump administration needs to do something quite impactful to gain votes. And, in my view, that may be lowering interest rates drastically, beyond what the data may suggest.

Looking Ahead

In the short term, I prefer to remain on the sidelines as there are no solid signs that suggest the end of the conflict.

Therefore, I see days like today as a dead cat bounce, despite the solid market breadth and returns across pretty much all the industries within the S&P 500:

From a price action perspective, I still believe the S&P 500 may see 6,150 in H1 this year, especially if next week, the (indirect) talks between the U.S. and Iran don't lead to a clear reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

That said, in the longer term, I remain constructive, despite the poor economic data that I presented in the first section of this article.

My main point is that the oil shock appears to be transitory, and the Fed will likely adopt a dovish stance, starting in June this year.

That said, if policy easing is delayed (which is what the market is currently pricing in), the stretched earnings expectations that I discussed in this article become harder to defend, and I wouldn't be surprised to see the S&P 500 selling off a second time this year.

data.title: First Solar: Sold Out U.S. Solar Leader At An Attractive Discount
data.description:
Intro

Given the ongoing Iran crisis and the resulting surge in oil prices, it’s natural for investors to look for potential beneficiaries in this elevated energy pricing environment. The most direct winners are, of course, oil producers, whose topline improves when crude prices rise. Oil is a global commodity, and although there are many regional blends priced differently, overall prices tend to advance and decline broadly in sync.

Natural gas, however, behaves differently. The market has been heavily disrupted as Qatar’s LNG exports are constrained, and roughly 20% of global LNG supply is effectively cut off due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Unlike oil, gas is transported around the world largely in the form of LNG, which means the ability to export depends on liquefaction capacity. In the United States, only about 14% of natural gas production can be liquefied and shipped to world markets.

Why does this matter when looking at the solar panel giant First Solar right now? The fact is, natural gas remains the main source of electricity generation in the U.S. Since U.S. gas prices do not move in sync with global LNG markets, the higher global price environment hasn’t translated into higher domestic gas prices. Instead, U.S. natural gas prices have even declined slightly since the Iran crisis began, while global gas prices have surged more than 50%.

EIA forecast for 2024–2027 US electricity generation by source:

FSLR’s position in that environment

This means First Solar, which primarily sells utility-scale solar modules domestically in the U.S., does not directly benefit from rising global gas prices. At first glance, it might seem like a winner in a high‑energy‑price environment, as it provides an alternative to traditional fossils, but in reality, it isn’t — at least not directly or in the short term.

Furthermore, after looking closely at First Solar, I found that their production is actually fully booked for the next three years. That means there’s no immediate benefit to the company from higher energy prices, even if the increased cost of fossil fuels could lead to greater demand for solar modules. They just don't have any available capacity to produce and sell more.

However, with the Iran/Hormuz crisis triggering a broad market selloff, including First Solar’s stock, it might be worth taking a closer look for a potential long-term secular winner available at a discounted price right now.

First Solar in Brief

Founded in 1999, First Solar is the leading U.S. solar technology company. The company stands out among the world’s ten largest solar manufacturers as the only one headquartered in the United States and the only major player that does not operate manufacturing facilities in China.

First Solar’s advanced thin‑film photovoltaic (PV) technology, developed in its R&D centers in California and Ohio, represents a new generation of solar solutions, offering a competitive, high‑performance, and lower‑carbon alternative to traditional crystalline‑silicon panels.

First Solar operates five manufacturing facilities in the United States — three in Ohio, one in Alabama, and one in Louisiana. These plants are expected to provide more than 17 GW of annual domestic manufacturing capacity once fully ramped. Globally, the company anticipates reaching approximately 25 GW of annual production capacity by 2026, supported by additional manufacturing sites in India, Malaysia, and Vietnam.

The company describes its manufacturing process as follows:

First Solar thin film PV modules are produced using a fully integrated, continuous process under one roof that does not rely on Chinese crystalline silicon (c-Si) supply chains. Its proprietary, vertically integrated process transforms sheets of glass into fully functioning solar panels in approximately four hours with a combination of highly skilled workers, Industry 4.0 architecture, machine-to-machine communication, artificial intelligence, and Internet of Things connectivity.

Advantages of First Solar technology over Chinese crystalline silicon (c-Si):

Each module features a layer of Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) semiconductor, derived from byproducts of copper and zinc mining, which boasts a number of qualities over conventional c-Si, including lower cost, superior scalability, and a higher theoretical efficiency limit.

First Solar’s thin film PV technology produces energy-efficient modules with a superior degradation rate, temperature coefficient, spectral and shading response, and the smallest environmental footprint in the industry. First Solar's thin film modules require only 1-2% of the semiconductor material needed by traditional c-Si modules to produce a comparable amount of power.

First Solar is the U.S. market leader, holding 12% of the utility solar market share, and the market is projected to grow by an average of 5.3% annually through 2034:

Q4 FY 2025 Earnings and 2026 Outlook

Q4 EPS missed expectations by $0.40, while revenue beat by $114 million.

Outlook fell well short of analysts’ expectations, coming in significantly below the $6.16 billion consensus estimate, with an estimated 17.0–18.2 GW of modules sold. The GAAP profit outlook wasn’t provided due to uncertainty surrounding tax credit discounts and other factors affecting the accounting.

The weak 2026 outlook has forced analysts to meaningfully reduce their expectations going forward:

The market reacted very negatively to the results, with First Solar's stock dropping nearly 15% the next trading day.

Valuation

To evaluate First Solar’s value, in my view, the forward P/E multiple gives a good read, as the company has $2.8 billion in cash on the balance sheet and only about $500 million in short‑ and long‑term debt.

With forward P/E multiples sitting at very low levels, ranging between 5 and 10 for the next five years, signaling clear undervaluation, it’s natural to wonder why. It could be at least partially blamed since the company issued a weak outlook and analysts cut their forecasts significantly, but it still seems, in my view, disproportionately discounted.

Why Market Discounts First Solar So Deeply

The reason is the transferable tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). These 45X advanced manufacturing production tax credits are generated by the company’s U.S.-based manufacturing facilities. In 2025, the company cashed in about $1.6 billion from these credits and expects to make an additional $2.10 to $2.19 billion from the Section 45X tax credit in 2026. So, if we look at 2026 EBITDA guidance, it is estimated to be $500 million to $590 million without these credits. With the 2026 capex projected to be in the range of $800 million to $1 billion, it means the company would actually lose money without government incentives.

So, these low P/E multiples are only relevant if the tax credit system remains unchanged going forward.

The explanation of these credits and their maturity:

The 45X Credit, established by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, is effective for components produced and sold after December 2022 but, for components other than critical minerals, will phase out starting in 2030 and terminate completely by 2033. The 45X Credit is designed to strengthen U.S. energy security by incentivizing the onshoring of production of key clean energy components and critical minerals.

The big question is, what happens when these credits start shrinking in 2030 and vanish entirely by 2033? Nobody really knows, and that uncertainty is probably the biggest reason for the steep discount in First Solar’s stock price right now. As shown in the Seeking Alpha analyst estimate table above, coverage dropped from 17 analysts in 2028 to just 6 in 2030, likely reflecting the unpredictability.

What we do know, listening to the latest earnings call and analysts' questions around the topic, is that normal gross margin without government tax credits is considered to be around 20%. If the company’s revenue is expected to reach $7.2 billion by 2030, that could translate to roughly $1.5 billion in gross margin. With other operating costs averaging around 10% of revenue, as it has been, a quick back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests about $700 million in operating profit. Plus, the 2030 tax incentives won’t be phased out yet, only starting to gradually decrease. I think while it’s tough to make truly accurate predictions, I could still expect profits to remain at least in the range of $700 million to $1.5 billion in 2030-2033, even if no new supportive tax measures are introduced by the U.S. administration.

Given First Solar’s unique position as the only U.S. solar cell producer at this scale, I believe the current restrictions on foreign imports are likely to remain, and even without administration tax support, this could create a strong pricing environment, enabling the company to enjoy better margins.

The Market Trends in Favor of First Solar

According to Electrek, writing at the end of 2025:

Renewables make up 26% of US generation

The mix of all renewables (wind, solar, hydropower, biomass, and geothermal) produced 8.7% more electricity in January-November than a year earlier and accounted for 25.7% of total US electricity production, up from 24.3% 12 months earlier.

Renewables’ share of electrical generation is now second to only that of natural gas, whose electrical output actually dropped by 3.7% during the first 11 months of 2025.

As reported by IEA, the overall electricity demand grows, and renewables are getting an extra boost from the decline in coal-fired capacity:

In 2026, U.S. coal generation declines by 7% in our forecast as generation from renewable sources increases and the electric power sector retires about 4% of its coal-fired generating capacity.

EIA foresees continued strong solar growth, with 41.5 GW of utility-scale solar capacity being added by the end of January 2027..

...and the growth of fossils is expected to stall:

.. forecasted growth for fossil fuels and nuclear power is even bleaker than that experienced last year. Zero growth is currently projected for nuclear power while net fossil fuel capacity is expected to fall by 4,261.3 MW.

Thus, during the coming 12 months, renewables and battery storage will account for all net new utility-scale capacity additions.

From a purely economic standpoint, the additional key factor is the cost of producing kWh without subsidies. In that comparison, solar, along with wind, comes out on top, according to Lazard:

In a base comparison, utility-scale solar and wind have the lowest LCOE of all sources. Utility-scale solar ranges from $0.038/kWh to $0.217/kWh, while onshore wind registers the lowest possible LCOE over the narrowest range, from $0.037/kWh to $0.086/kWh. Offshore wind’s LCOE ranges between $72/MWh and $140/MWh.

For comparison, under the same criteria, gas peaking costs $0.149/kWh to $0.251/kWh, nuclear is $0.141/kWh to $0.220/kWh, coal is $0.071/kWh to $0.173/kWh, and gas combined cycle is $0.048/kWh to $0.109/kWh,

In short, while headlines focus on the current US administration’s fossil-fuel-friendly stance and the widespread belief that natural gas will be the main source to power the growing energy needs of AI-driven data centers, the reality paints a bit different picture. Utilities are shifting to renewables, and it seems to be the only segment of power generation expected to grow heading into 2026.

Risks

The main risk, as the company itself has noted, is regulatory uncertainty. While this could eventually turn out to be a net positive, the many variables involved make it tough for management to chart long-term strategies in such conditions. That said, its scale, unique technology, and leadership position in the U.S.—especially given widely communicated policies aimed at reducing Chinese exposure in solar markets—give the company a strong hand in dealings with the government.

After listening to the earnings call, it seems clear that the company’s international operations are becoming a meaningful concern as well. As management discussed, the India subsidiary has found its footing in the domestic market—operating at full utilization and roughly breakeven—but the Vietnam and Malaysia facilities tell a very different story. These factories are significantly underutilized, currently running at only about 20% capacity.

Management largely attributes this to ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and other regulatory policies. However, it also appears that the company has not yet identified the right strategic role for these overseas factories. Until that happens, these operations represent an additional layer of risk for the business, as I see it.

Takeaway

While looking for beneficiaries of high energy prices, I came across what I see as a clear energy sector secular winner: First Solar. It is essentially the only large‑scale U.S.‑based solar technology manufacturer with unique, IP‑protected technology, which gives it a rare and strategically important position in the market.

The main reason the company is not trading at higher multiples is understandable. Once the current tax credits expire in 2033, there’s uncertainty about how the political landscape and policies might develop. However, both parties have seemingly agreed on one point: the U.S. must reduce its dependence on China‑linked supply chains, especially in strategic sectors like energy.

At the same time, electricity demand is rising —driven by hyperscale AI data centers, and solar remains one of the fastest‑growing energy sources. First Solar continues to evolve to become more efficient and cost‑effective at scale. I think this creates a realistic path for the company to remain profitable even without government incentives.

I rate First Solar as a Buy at current levels.

data.title: Myriad Genetics: SOTP Suggests The Company Is Undervalued
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Vatsal Garg as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Company Overview

Myriad Genetics, Inc. (MYGN) is a molecular diagnostics and precision medicine company. Its products are used by doctors and patients to help answer important medical questions, such as who may be at higher risk of cancer, which treatment may work better, how prenatal care should be managed, and which psychiatric medicines may be more suitable for a patient. Over time, the company has expanded beyond its original Hereditary Cancer focus.

Myriad reported four main product categories – 1. Hereditary Cancer which generated $372.4 million of revenue, 2. Tumor Profiling which generated $121.7 million in revenue, 3. Prenatal that generated $186.3 million, and 4. Mental Health generated $144.1 million. Total revenue was $824.5 million, according to Myriad’s 2025 Annual Report.

Myriad generates cash by developing tests, running those tests in labs, delivering them to the doctors and patients, and then getting paid through the insurance reimbursement, patient payments, or a mix of both. The business depends on science as well as coverage policies, doctor workflow adoption, EMR integration, number of patients accessing them, total billing and collections, and efficiency of the process.

Evolution of the Company: 2019 to March 2026

Myriad changed a lot over the last several years, but the stock did not benefit from that change. In 2018, the company bought Counsyl, which added a meaningful Prenatal business and pushed Myriad further beyond its original Hereditary Cancer focus. By FY 2019, the company was still profitable, with $851.1 million of revenue and ~$4.4 million of net income. The business mix was already becoming more diversified.

That changed in 2020, when COVID sharply hurt the volumes and profitability. Revenue fell to $638.6 million, and net loss widened to about $199.6 million. The goodwill and intangible impairment reached $99.7 million. In 2021, management launched a strategic transformation plan focused on Oncology, Women’s Health, and Mental Health, while also selling older side businesses such as MyPath, RBM, and the Autoimmune / Vectra Assets. Revenue improved to $690.6 million, but the company still reported a net loss of $27.2 million.

In 2022, Myriad continued reshaping the business through automation, new labs, commercial changes, and newer products such as FirstGene and Precise MRD. It also acquired Gateway Genomics. Even so, total revenue was only $678.4 million and net loss widened to $112.0 million. In 2023, revenue improved to $753.2 million, but profitability still did not recover. The company also raised $117.6 million through an equity offering. In 2024, the business looked stronger on the surface, with revenue rising to $837.6 million.

But later that year, UnitedHealthCare changed coverage for certain multi-gene pharmacogenetic tests, including GeneSight. Myriad said about $45 million of 2024 GeneSight revenue was exposed to UHC, and it also recorded a $43 million GeneSight technology impairment. By 2025, management was positioning the company as a broader precision medicine platform built around Cancer Care, Prenatal Health, and Mental Health. Revenue was $824.5 million, but Mental Health revenue fell 15%, and the net loss widened to $365.9 million. The Impairment charges reached $319.4 million, and operating cash flow was only $1.8 million. Taken together, the company broadened its portfolio over time, but the stock was not rewarded for that change.

Recent Performance: Positives and Limits

In recent results, Myriad has shown that not all parts of the business are weak. Test volumes have grown in some important areas even when revenue has been under pressure. Hereditary Cancer Testing remained one of the strongest parts of the portfolio (rising 7% in volumes), and management also highlighted resilience in Prolaris. The company has also been working on cost control. Lower reagent and supply costs helped reduce cost per test, and losses narrowed even as gross margins stayed fairly stable. These are the real positives and show that the core business still has strength.

At the same time, these positives do not fully remove the bigger concerns. While revenue in Mental Health’s volume rose 6%, revenue remained under pressure and fell 15% because reimbursement changes reduced average revenue per test. Some newer or smaller business lines still appear less profitable than the core hereditary franchise. So, while Myriad is showing some operating improvement, the market still seems unwilling to give the whole company a stronger valuation. And this is because the business mix remains uneven and cash flow is still weak.

Public Channel Checks

Public channel checks suggest that the lab supply chain is not the main problem. In 2025, the cost of revenue was $247.9 million on $824.5 million of revenue. This was about 30% of the sales. Management said cost per test improved because reagent and supply costs were lower, and it also indicates that current facilities provide enough testing capacity. This suggests that lab capacity and supply chain are probably not the main reasons the stock has remained weak.

The bigger problem appears to be reimbursement and workflow execution. Mental Health is the clearest example. Even though volume rose 6% in 2025. Revenue fell 15% because average revenue per test declined after the UHC coverage change. That shows the issue is not demand alone, but how much the company is able to collect per test.

According to the fourth quarter 2025 earnings release, Prenatal's fourth-quarter volume fell 6% because of transition issues after the new order management system. In the same release, management said Hereditary Oncology volume rose 9%, and unaffected Hereditary Cancer volume in Women’s Health rose 11%.

Reason the Stock Trades at a Discount

There are several reasons for the discount:

Weak cash flow generation

In 2025, Myriad reported $824.5 million of revenue, $576.6 million of gross profit, $38.9 million of adjusted EBITDA, $1.8 million of operating cash flow, and a GAAP-based net loss of $365.9 million according to Myriad’s 2025 Annual Report. This is not enough to support a strong multiple rating for the stock.

Reimbursement Fragility

The UHC-GeneSight decision hurt investor confidence not only in Mental Health but also in the broader business model.

Portfolio Complexity

As with any conglomerate structure, the market is discounting a mixture where you have one great asset followed by a decent asset and then one small and strategic asset and finally a damaged one with liabilities.

Repeated strategic shifts and turnaround efforts appear to have weakened investor confidence in management.

Peer Analysis

Myriad generated $824.5 million of 2025 revenue with an equity value of $446 million. This implies 0.52x EV/Sales and 10.9x EV/EBITDA.

For comparison, Natera, Inc. (NTRA) generated $2.3 billion in revenue and has a market value of ~$22 billion, implying the EV/Sales 9.1x.

Further, Guardant Health, Inc. (GH) generated $982 million in revenue with a market value of $7.8 billion, implying 7x EV/Sales.

NeoGenomics, Inc. (NEO), generated $727 million in revenue and trades at ~1.6x EV/Sales and ~27.4x EV/Adj. EBITDA. Castle Biosciences, Inc. (CSTL) generated $344.2 million of revenue and trades at ~1.1x EV/Sales and ~8.4xEV/Adj. EBITDA.

The current valuation suggests that the market is still applying a discount for portfolio complexity and weak cash conversion.

Detailed SOTP Valuation

Myriad does not disclose its EBITDA by business line, although it does disclose revenue by product category. So, we went for analytical estimates for divisional EBITDA based on the $38.9 million of adjusted EBITDA from Myriad’s 2025 Annual Reports. In 2025, category revenue was $372.4 million for Hereditary Cancer, $121.7 million for Tumor Profiling, $186.3 million for Prenatal and $144.1 million for Mental Health for total revenue of $824.5 million.

Because Myriad does not report EBITDA by business line, the profitability split below is our estimate based on the quality and position of each business. We give Hereditary Cancer the highest margin because it is the strongest and most established part of the company. We give Prenatal a small positive margin because it looks useful but not very strong. We treat Tumor Profiling as near break-even because it is still small and not scaled enough. We treat Mental Health as loss-making because reimbursement pressure has hurt revenue per test. We then deduct shared company costs separately so that the full bridge matches the company’s reported adjusted EBITDA.

A reasonable profitability split is as follows:

Hereditary Cancer appears clearly profitable with (est.) EBITDA margin of ~22% or about $82 million of EBITDA [my estimate]. Tumor Profiling looks near break-even to slightly loss-making at -$2 million [our estimate]. Prenatal appears to be modestly profitable, or about $13 million [my estimate]. Mental Health appears loss-making, or about -$17 million [my estimate]. Corporate and shared costs appear to be ~-$37 million [my estimate]. This bridge sums to about $39 million, broadly in line with reported adjusted EBITDA.

The impairment record supports the view that Women’s Health and Mental Health are the main stress points. According to Myriad’s Annual Report 2025, the company recorded $234.7 million of goodwill impairment, split between Women's Health ($143.5 million) and Mental Health ($91.2 million). In 2024, according to Myriad’s Annual Report 2024, total goodwill and long-lived asset impairment charges were $56.8 million, including a $43.0 million GeneSight developed-technology impairment after the UHC policy change.

We apply conservative multiples to each business based on maturity, scale, reimbursement risk, and peer context. Hereditary Cancer receives the highest multiple because it is the strongest and most established franchise. Tumor Profiling receives a lower multiple because it remains subscale. Prenatal receives a moderate multiple because it appears commercially useful but less differentiated. Mental Health receives the lowest multiple because reimbursement pressure has already reduced revenue per test and led to impairment charges.

Using the conservative scale-based values, Hereditary Cancer is worth about $466 million (using 1.25x post-discount EV/Sales). Tumor Profiling unit is worth about $91 million (using 0.75x post-discount EV/Sales). Prenatal is worth about $177 million (using 0.95x post-discount EV/sales). Mental Health is worth about $36 million (using a heavily discounted EV/sales multiple of 0.25x).

Together this produces a gross SOTP value of $770 million.

That said, we also need to adjust the other expenses for running the company, such as lab overhead, public company costs, IT, R&D costs, etc. We apply a $225 million conglomerate and stranded-cost haircut to reflect shared lab overhead, public-company costs, IT, R&D, and other central expenses that would not disappear fully in a simpler structure.

So, the Net Operating Value comes out to be $545 million. Adding back the net cash of $20.8 million brings the equity's estimated value to $566 million.

Using 93.5 million shares outstanding, the implied value is about $6.05 per share.

Risks and Catalysts

Myriad still has some clear risks. The biggest risk is reimbursement pressure, especially in mental health, where lower revenue per test has already hurt results. Another risk is that some of the smaller or newer business lines may still not be earning enough profit. The company also has weak free cash flow, and that makes investors cautious. If new products take longer to scale or if payer pressure spreads to other parts of the business, the stock could stay weak.

At the same time, there are also clear catalysts. Hereditary Cancer testing remains the strongest part of the business and has shown better resilience than other units. The company is also moving forward with commercial testing for select customers in HCT Precise MRD. This could help the market view the oncology side more positively if adoption improves. Another possible catalyst is the planned AI-enabled Prolaris launch in Q2 2026. Cost cutting, and lower reagent and supply costs have also helped improve cost per test. Finally, if the company ever chooses to simplify the portfolio further, that could create additional upside.

Takeaways

The thesis is simple; the market currently sees a bundled business with uneven assets and weak consolidated cash flow and reimbursement pressure in one of its segments. That has pushed the stock to a low valuation. On a conservative SOTP basis, however, the shares already appear undervalued even without assuming a major strategic change. If the company executes well on its 2026 product and operating catalysts, the valuation gap could begin to narrow. Further portfolio simplification could create additional upside.

Myriad appears undervalued on a conservative SOTP basis. Its estimated value of about $6.05 per share is above the current market price. The market still seems focused on reimbursement pressure, weak cash flow, and portfolio complexity while not fully recognizing the strength of the Hereditary Cancer franchise. There are also real catalysts in 2026 – including commercial testing for select customers in HCT Precise MRD, the planned AI-enabled Prolaris launch, and ongoing cost discipline. A simpler portfolio could create further upside, but even without that, the stock already looks inexpensive.

data.title: The Market's Big Problem Is Persistence
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

Where Persistence Has Become A Big Problem

If you’ve been following this article over the last several weeks, you may have noticed a theme that focuses on the importance of time as an influence on market behavior.

  • The market's time in a trend period has an effect.
  • Investors' patience with the trend period has an effect.
  • Investors’ reactions to time are often gradual, then excessive.

Time in a trend affects sentiment, positioning, and more.

The fundamental factors behind the market’s trend are also likely to be affected over time, as is the case with the persistence of the Iran war and the elevated price of oil, both together and independently.

Few would argue that the higher price of oil, driven by the war, has depressed stock prices and expectations for global economic growth, but how quickly the market and economies will recover should the war come to an end in the next week, month, quarter, or longer is debatable.

However, the longer the war goes on, keeping oil prices elevated, oil production declining, and the flow of oil to importing countries curtailed, the bigger the problem becomes.

Higher oil prices are an obvious problem, so when the war ends, the next obvious question will be, "When and for how long will oil prices fall?"

There’s never one reason for a price trend, but below you’ll find a visual analysis of the relationship between the changes and levels of OECD Oil Inventory Data and the price of crude oil. Note that this data excludes strategic reserves, in order to put more focus on the open market source of oil.

As you can see, there are three measures.

The top chart shows the level of commercial stocks (inventory excluding strategic reserves) and the price of oil. It’s clear that the price of oil is inversely correlated with the level and trend of inventories.

The middle chart shows month-to-month changes in inventory along with a three-month average of the monthly changes and the price of oil. Again, the price of oil is highly correlated with the 3-month average trend and level of inventory changes.

The bottom row has two charts that give the correlation data for the one-month period and the total level. Here you can see clearly that one-month changes aren’t a problem; however, the cumulative impact over time is highly correlated.

A persistent trend of declining inventories is a problem that leads to persistently higher oil prices.

In case you didn’t notice, the reporting of data lags by a couple of months. So, while this is the most recent data, it doesn’t include the impact of the war.

How Will This Market Bottom?

There are three common ways market corrections and bear markets bottom out.

  1. The first is quick and violent capitulation;
  2. The second is a process of sector rotation; and
  3. The third is through a long depression of investor sentiment, combined with compressed price action.

The investors and the media are looking out for capitulation (number 1), but the market is saying it prefers rotation right now.

In other words, while the market is trading like a bear market and the media’s reports often sound dire, the market is not selling everything in a rush of capitulation.

If you’re only looking at the S&P 500 or NASDAQ 100, it may feel like capitulation is underway, but the broader market tells a different story.

As a result, the process of sector rotation can indicate when the market has bottomed and/or when the market slides into a state of capitulation.

When Will This Market Bottom?

In last week’s Market Outlook, we introduced the PRIME framework for analyzing a market’s trend to determine whether it is likely to continue or reverse.

As with most good frameworks or methodologies, it has basic principles you can use immediately to improve your market analysis. Additionally, with experience, you’ll discover several levels of detail that make the PRIME method even more powerful.

The basics look like this.

The PRIME framework is based on 5 primary conditions that influence a stock’s or market’s trend. They are as follows:

  1. Price trends and patterns: Market moves create and enhance emotions like fear, greed, confidence, hope, etc.
  2. Relative performance: The best- and worst-performing stocks attract investors’ attention.
  3. Institutional buying and selling: The largest, most durable trends are driven by institutions.
  4. Momentum: Prices and investor emotions have a measurable momentum factor that contributes to the strength of a trend.
  5. Expected Earnings Growth: The foundational reason for owning a stock is based on a belief that the company will grow its earnings and value.

The PRIME method provides a simple way to measure all five elements:

  1. Price Trend & Patterns: Market Phases indicators
  2. Relative Performance: Triple Play Leadership indicators
  3. Institutional Accumulation: Triple Play Volume Trend indicators
  4. Momentum: Real Motion indicators
  5. Expected Earnings or A Growth Story: This is more subjective.

This market will have established a reliable bottom when three of the four top PRIME criteria are bullish in the indexes, and/or when several growth-oriented sectors are also in bullish conditions based on the PRIME criteria.

Here are a few sector charts explained.

XLE, the energy ETF, is a good example of what a clear breakout looks like with all the PRIME indicators in a bullish condition. The charts that follow are not very bullish, but they are showing signs of holding or turning in a market where the indexes (SPY and QQQ) are very bearish.

XLP, staples, is a defensive sector, but it’s holding its 200 DMA while the market trades much lower than its 200 MA. Real Motion moving averages often identify support areas, so the Real Motion chart on the 200 MA is significant.

XLB, materials, has moved over its 10 DMA, and the Real Motion 10/50 momentum indicator’s green dots confirm there may be enough momentum to continue higher.

Additionally, the leadership indicator has a bullish reading, which suggests the sector’s improving price action is not a result of following the market. This conclusion is easy and obvious in the current bearish market environment, but when the market is trending higher, a leadership indicator with this pattern highlights the strongest trends.

XLU, utilities, broke down below the important support shown by the dotted line. However, it held its 50 DMA and maintained its strong leadership condition. If XLU trades over its 10 DMA and the resistance of the breakdown level, Real Motion is in a position to turn bullish, which would create a very bullish chart.

Stocks That Can Do Well Now and When The War Ends

One way to apply the PRIME method is to focus on stocks in leading trends with a compelling growth story.

The AI-driven trend of data center construction has created enormous growth prospects for the fiberoptics industry. Three companies that are well-positioned to experience strong growth for the foreseeable future in this industry are GLW (Corning, Inc.), CIEN (Ciena Corp.) and AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics, Inc.).

Despite the war and weak market, these stocks have had large moves higher over the last month. As you’ll see in their charts below, they all pushed higher with “all bullish” PRIME conditions.

Timing trades is based on price patterns and trading strategies. The PRIME indicators highlight when the conditions for a strong trend or reversals exist.

In the last week, these stocks have begun to pull back, and some of the PRIME conditions have turned bearish.

This creates opportunities to buy a correction in a strong long-term trend when the intermediate- and/or longer-term trend resumes its upmove as defined by the PRIME indicators.

Below you’ll find a quick analysis similar to what we did on the sector charts above.

Corning, Inc.

GLW has a strong growth story.

  • Strong revenue acceleration in 2025 - FY2025 revenue surged 19% to $15.6B, the best growth in years, driven by the Optical Communications segment benefiting from AI data center buildout. Estimates call for FY2026 revenue of $18.8B (+20%) and FY2027 of $21.4B (+14%).
  • EPS poised for a major step-up - Consensus FY2026 EPS of $5.09 represents a 174% increase over FY2025 GAAP EPS of $1.86, reflecting both operating leverage and absence of one-time charges. The 5-year CAGR (FY2020–FY2025) stands at 28.0% on EPS.
  • Reasonable valuation given growth trajectory - Forward P/E of 28.7x on FY2026e EPS and P/S of 8.0x TTM revenue are moderate for a company with 20%+ revenue growth and expanding margins. The trailing P/E of 80x reflects depressed FY2024/2025 GAAP earnings.
  • Consistent quarterly EPS beats - All four recent quarters showed adjusted EPS beats vs. consensus, with revenue generally in-line. Q3 2025 GAAP EPS was impacted by a non-cash impairment charge (negative GAAP EPS), but adjusted EPS remained positive.

Its technicals have deteriorated, but they could recover quickly.

The weakness in the market over the last two days has pulled GLW back under the 10 DMA (magenta) and down to the 50 DMA.

Warning: Both Real Motion indicators have red dots (bearish) under their 50 DMA, while the price chart sits over its 50 DMA. This condition suggests that if the price breaks its 50 DMA, it will continue lower.

However, if price moves back over its 10 DMA and the Real Motion charts turn green, everything will be in gear for a new move higher.

Applied Optoelectronics, Inc.

  • Explosive revenue growth in 2025 - Full-year 2025 revenue surged 83% to $455.7M from $249.4M in 2024, driven by strong data center demand for optical interconnects. Consensus estimates project ~$962M in 2026 (+111%) and ~$1.88B in 2027 (+95%).
  • Company approaching profitability - While all historical EPS figures are negative, AAOI posted only -$0.04 EPS in Q4 2025 and analysts estimate positive net income beginning in Q2 2026 (~$0.06/share). Full-year 2026 consensus EPS estimate is ~$0.95.
  • Elevated P/S valuation reflects high growth expectations - At a price/sales ratio of 18.9x on TTM revenue, the stock is priced for continued hyper-growth. P/E, PEG, and forward multiples are not meaningful given recent losses.

AAOI has a very similar situation as GLW above, but its momentum is stronger, and it’s further from its 50 DMA.

If it climbs back over its 10 DMA and the $100 level that has been pivotal, the price action will suggest it’s ready to move higher. If that happens with bullish reading in leadership, volume, and Real Motion, it will have all the ingredients of an explosive growth stock.

Ciena Inc.

CIEN’s growth story is strong, but it’s led by its sales growth:

  • Explosive EPS recovery expected in FY2026 - Consensus estimates call for EPS of $7.48 in FY2026 vs. $0.87 in FY2025, a ~760% surge. This reflects anticipated acceleration from AI-driven data center buildouts and strong demand for coherent optical networking infrastructure.
  • Revenue inflection in progress - 5yr Sales CAGR of 6.2% understates recent momentum. FY2025 revenue of $4.77B was up 19% YoY, and TTM revenue reached $5.12B. Consensus projects $6.1B in FY2026 (+28%) and $7.35B in FY2027 (+20%).
  • Consistent EPS misses, strong revenue beats - In each of the last 4 quarters, actual EPS came in below consensus estimates. Revenue, however, beat expectations in all 4 quarters, suggesting strong top line execution but elevated cost structures weighing on GAAP profitability.
  • Rich valuation discounts near-term earnings, prices in a recovery - At a P/E of 275x on trailing EPS of $1.59, the stock price of $437.70 is almost entirely forward-looking. The forward P/E of 58.5x (on FY2026 est. EPS of $7.48) and P/S of 12.1x reflect high investor confidence in the AI capex tailwind.

Its technical setup is also very strong

CIEN has had a very consistently strong 2026, as seen by its leadership and volume trend indicators, having a solid trend, and the red line remaining well above the blue line.

The recent pullback under the 10 DMA has landed right on a good level to bottom - the support of the $360 level, which was its February swing high.

Leadership and volume trend remain bullish, and while the Real Motion momentum measures have turned negative, they are at levels that are likely to be support. If CIEN gets back over its 10 DMA and $400, it’s likely that everything will be lined up for a continued move higher.

As the market corrects, you can see if these three stocks and other market leaders are ready to resume their uptrend by looking at how their PRIME trends hold up or deteriorate.

Best wishes for your trading.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Despite markets making new lows and broad risk-off signals dominating - rising volatility, weak breadth, sector rotation into defensives, and higher rates - there are modest signs of stabilization with improving internals, resilient “Modern Family” components, and relative strength outside the U.S. Overall, conditions remain pressured and oversold with a bearish tilt, but divergences and seasonal tailwinds suggest the potential for a near-term bounce rather than continued straight-line downside.

Neutral

  • With the market making new lows, the market internals are giving a weak divergent reading with up-down volume and advance-decline improving week over week and the McClellan Oscillator holding last week's levels. (=)
  • The risk gauge is showing neutral with the relative strength in rates and weakness in metals. (=)
  • The Modern Family held up relatively well this week, holding last week's lows across the board while the broader market broke down. Many are still outperforming the S&P and oversold on Real Motion. (=)
  • Emerging and developed markets are trending lower but put in more of an inside week and held up better than the US markets. (=)
  • Seasonally, we are past the typical February and March weakness, and April tends to pick up. (=)

Risk-Off

  • Volume patterns lean heavily towards distribution in all except IWM, which is more neutral. (-)
  • Three of the four indexes made new recent lows on the move this week. They are each below their 200-Day Moving Average and oversold on real motion. (-)
  • Sectors skewed risk-off with Technology and Financials hit the worst, while risk-off sectors like Utilities and consumer staples were up. (-)
  • New high new low closed slightly above last week’s levels, though still a negative reading overall. (-)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) continue to trend lower. (-)
  • Volatility made a new high this week, the highest reading going back to April 2025. It is overbought, though not at extreme levels. (-)
  • Growth plummeted this week even as value maintained its relative value and is still holding its 200-Day Moving Average. (-)
  • Soft commodities push higher, signalling inflationary pressure. (-)
  • Oil closed at a new high with continued concern over Middle East conflict. (-)
  • Rates continued to trend higher as the Fed is handcuffed and markets are expecting higher rates. (-)

Actionable Trading Plan

Core Positioning (Bias: Risk-Off but Oversold Bounce Potential)

  • Maintain a defensive posture overall, but be prepared to tactically deploy into a short-term bounce rather than pressing shorts at current extremes.
  • Favor value over growth and defensive sectors (utilities, staples), while growth remains weak and below key trend structures.

What to Do Now

  • Reduce or avoid new short exposure unless markets re-accelerate lower - conditions are already stretched with volatility elevated.
  • Look to scale into strength (not weakness) in areas showing relative resilience: select “Modern Family” leaders and non-U.S. equities that held up on the breakdown.
  • Keep position sizes smaller than normal given elevated volatility and unstable breadth.

Bounce Strategy (Primary Opportunity)

  • Expect a reflex rally driven by oversold conditions + seasonal tailwind (April).
  • On a bounce, sell into strength in weak sectors (tech, financials) rather than chasing upside.
  • Use the bounce to rebalance toward stronger themes (defensive/value, commodities with inflation tailwinds).

Risk Management

  • Respect that rates + oil + inflation pressures = headwind for equities, so avoid overcommitting to risk.
  • If volatility continues to expand or breadth fails to confirm a bounce, tighten exposure quickly and raise cash.
  • Stay flexible - this is a trading environment, not a trend-following one right now.

Tactical Tilt

  • Slight overweight: defensives, value, select commodities
  • Underweight: growth, high beta, rate-sensitive equities

Bottom Line

Play for a short-term bounce within a broader risk-off regime, using strength to de-risk and reposition rather than assuming a durable trend reversal.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SCHG: Strong Companies At An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Investment And Thesis

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (ETF) that aims to provide exposure to the growth segment of U.S. large-cap equities. This exposure is achieved by tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index with meaningful concentration across technology and communication services.

SCHG is down 13% year-to-date and 16.7% from its all-time high, primarily due to multiple compression. This is due to rising macroeconomic uncertainty stemming from geopolitical tensions as well as tariff policy uncertainties. That pressure has created an attractive entry point for SCHG, in my opinion. The fund’s top holdings all have strong revenue growth, expanding profit margins, and expected strong forward earnings growth. As a result, I rate SCHG a Buy.

Fund Overview

SCHG tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, which is produced by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and licensed to Schwab. The index is constructed via a screening process.

First, the top 750 U.S. publicly traded companies by market capitalization are ranked in order. This ensures the fund solely focuses on large-cap with no small- or mid-cap exposure.

Second, each security is scored on six factors with an equal mix of historical performance, current earnings, and expected future growth. These factors are projected price-to-earnings ratio (P/E), projected earnings per share (EPS) growth, price-to-book ratio (P/B), dividend yield, trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue growth, and TTM EPS growth. Finally, the companies that exhibit promising relative growth are included in the fund and weighted by market capitalization.

In addition, SCHG rebalances on a quarterly basis. Its quarterly rebalance is simply adjusting the weight of the portfolio, which ensures no stock exceeds 24% of the fund and that stocks that are above 4.5% weight collectively cannot exceed 48% of the fund. This ensures the fund does not solely focus on a few stocks that experience a surge in price, such as Nvidia (NVDA).

SCHG charges an expense ratio of 0.04%, which is lower than most funds due to its passive tracking nature and securities lending, which assists in lowering costs. This ETF is also very liquid, with an average daily volume of 21 million shares.

SCHG also distributes a small dividend, with a TTM dividend yield of 0.45%. This negligible dividend is by design, as SCHG’s top holdings have historically returned capital to shareholders via buybacks rather than dividends. In addition, as this fund focuses on growth-oriented equities, these companies rarely distribute large dividends as they focus on their underlying business.

SCHG is also relatively tax efficient, as portfolio turnover is on the lower end at 19%. As a passively managed fund, SCHG is not frequently selling positions and realizing capital gains that would otherwise be passed through to shareholders. In addition, when SCHG rebalances, it is typically adjusting weights rather than replacing holdings, further limiting taxable events. This assists in tax efficiency to reduce the drag of annual capital gains distributions.

Portfolio Holdings & Valuation

As of March 28, 2026, SCHG holds 196 individual equities, with their top 10 holdings accounting for 57.36% of total assets. Looking at their top 5 holdings, we can see that Nvidia, Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG) make up 43.19% of the fund. While these companies have delivered consistent significant growth and expect continued growth, they have experienced steep drawdowns as part of a broader market selloff, primarily driven by tariff uncertainty and geopolitical tensions.

I compiled the table above to present forward EPS estimates sourced from Seeking Alpha and calculated the respective forward P/E, estimated forward EPS growth, and forward PEG ratios for each of the top holdings.

While all of their holdings are expected to deliver significant growth in the next twelve months, we can see that NVDA stands out on a valuation basis. With a forward PE of 15x and a forward PEG of 0.12, we can see the market appears to be significantly underpricing its growth trajectory, despite consistent commentary from CEO Jensen Huang. Huang has recently spoken publicly about visibility to $1 trillion in revenue due to strong demand for its Blackwell and Vera Rubin Graphic Processing Units (GPU). As emphasized by the hyperscalers (MSFT, GOOG, and AMZN), supply is nowhere near demand.

Conversely, AAPL appears to carry the highest relative valuation with a forward PE of 26.7x and a forward PEG of 1.08. While their PEG carries a premium, AAPL is still expected to deliver strong earnings growth through its expanding services segment, a recovery in China phone volumes, and executing record share buybacks due to their strong free cash flow.

Finally, the three hyperscalers, trading at approximately 18-21x forward earnings, are expected to deliver strong earnings growth driven by re-acceleration in their respective cloud businesses and operating margin expansion as cloud pricing strengthens and capital expenditures begin to generate returns on investment.

Performance & Peer Comparison

I’ve compiled a table that compares SCHG to similar funds such as VUG and QQQ due to their overlapping exposure to technology stocks and similar investment philosophies.

Looking at total return, we can see that SCHG has delivered competitive returns, delivering a 3-year CAGR of 27.37%, a 5-year CAGR of 14.48%, and a 10-year CAGR of 18.32%. We can see that SCHG has outperformed VUG across every time horizon other than a year and has slightly underperformed QQQ on a 1, 3, and 5-year time horizon. In addition, we can see that SCHG has tracked their benchmark well, with only a few basis points lost to tracking error.

Risks

As of March 28, 2026, the current macroeconomic background is volatile with several near-term headwinds. President Donald Trump has maintained and escalated his position on tariffs, threatening supply chains. In addition, the U.S. continues its conflict with Iran, resulting in sustained elevated oil prices. This increase in energy costs further threatens supply chains while contributing to continued inflation, reducing the chance of rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. This environment is typically hostile for growth-oriented equities, which are more sensitive to higher discount rates.

According to CME FedWatch, the current market consensus is a 74.2% chance of no rate change, a 22.5% chance of one rate hike, and a 2% chance of two rate hikes. A higher rate environment is not favorable for high-beta names such as technology companies. For a fund like SCHG that focuses on growth-oriented equities, this presents a meaningful headwind for future growth.

Balance Sheet & FCF Risk

In addition, we can see that forward free cash flow in SCHG’s top holdings is expected to meaningfully contract, driven by accelerating AI infrastructure spend, particularly on NVDA's GPUs. I’ve compiled a table of the hyperscalers’ expected capital expenditures, TTM Operating Cash Flows, and the implied Free Cash Flow (FCF). As we can see, AMZN and GOOG are expected to produce negative FCF, with MSFT quickly approaching negative FCF. Investors who have historically relied on large-cap growth names to funnel high levels of FCF into share buybacks should temper their expectations as ambitious buildout plans take center stage.

As a result of this expected negative FCF, we can see that hyperscalers need additional capital to fund their capex. As a result, Amazon and Alphabet have raised debt to fund their ambitions. Amazon raised $54 billion in the U.S. and European bond markets, while Alphabet has tapped the bond market for nearly $32 billion. While both companies boast investment-grade credit ratings supported by their strong underlying business fundamentals, this is still a risk that needs to be monitored. Should the AI infrastructure buildout fail to generate meaningful returns on capital, the cost of carrying this debt can present a meaningful headwind.

Concentration Risk

SCHG is extremely concentrated, with the top 5 holdings making up 43% of the fund and the top 10 constituting 57%. Investors who purchase SCHG will be deeply exposed to AI and technology. In the event data centers fail to generate meaningful returns on capital, there is an outsized risk of steep drawdowns driven by investor pessimism. As an example, a 30% decline in NVDA alone would reduce SCHG’s NAV by roughly 3.5%. A 30% decline in AAPL would further reduce NAV by an additional 3.0%.

Who Should Buy SCHG?

SCHG is best suited for investors who are seeking growth-oriented companies, have a high-risk tolerance, and are bullish on the ongoing buildout of AI infrastructure. It is not suited for income-focused investors, as the fund’s TTM dividend yield of 0.45% is negligible. It is also not suitable for investors seeking international exposure, as the fund focuses on U.S. equities. SCHG is also a tax-efficient vehicle appropriate for many investment accounts, such as a taxable brokerage account, 401k, and IRA.

Conclusion

The fundamental bull cases for these companies remain durable. AI-driven capital expenditure by hyperscalers such as MSFT, AMZN, and GOOG remains strong, with all hyperscalers guiding for significant YoY growth in capex, a sign that AI demand continues to expand. NVDA continues to execute at the hardware and software layer, with strong visibility to $1 trillion in revenue. Every AI company continues to assert that the need for accelerated compute and inference infrastructure remains supply constrained and demand continues to be relentless. With SCHG trading approximately 16.7% from its ATH, forward P/Es on SCHG’s top holdings have compressed to an attractive level in my opinion. For investors with a long time horizon who are bullish on AI, I believe SCHG to be a Buy.

data.title: KBR: Low Valuation And Healthy Long-Term Drivers Make It A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

KBR (KBR) is trading at a significant discount versus historical levels. Its current forward P/E is over 40% lower than the 5-year historical average. While there are near-term headwinds due to the winding down of the Plaquemines LNG project in 2027 and geopolitical developments impacting Middle East projects, I believe these concerns are already getting priced in at current levels.

In the medium to long term, the company is well positioned to benefit from a strong focus on energy security globally and increasing defense spending. In the STS business, the company may get additional work to repair and rebuild war-related damages to energy infrastructure. Some of the delayed LNG projects, like the Lake Charles LNG project, may find financial backers due to the energy security theme. In MTS business, the company should benefit from increased defense logistics spending, and recent M&As like LinQuest should also help.

The stock is trading at a P/E in high single digits versus high teens historical valuations. Given good medium-term growth prospects and low valuations, I rate the stock a buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

I last covered KBR in June 2024, and it has not performed well since then. The company has seen TRANSCOM contract termination, and some of the LNG projects, like the Lake Charles Project, also got delayed. In Q4 2025, KBR’s revenue declined by 11% y/y to $1.9 bn. In the Mission Technology Solutions segment, revenue declined by 19% y/y due to a reduction in contingency EUCOM scope, NASA funding restrictions, new award delays, and procurement delays across US Government Defense and Intelligence clients. In the Sustainable Technology Solutions (STS) segment, revenues were down 2% y/y due to a decline in petrochemical capex and a pause in some green projects.

Looking ahead, while KBR faces some near-term headwinds, I remain optimistic about the company’s longer-term revenue prospects.

In the Sustainable Technology Solutions (STS) segment, recent backlog trends are encouraging. The company reported approximately 5% yr/yr growth in STS backlog, reaching around $4.2 billion at the end of fiscal 2025. Its book-to-bill was also a solid ~1.6x. Order trends and backlog are leading indicators of revenues, and this growth should support healthy revenue growth in 2026. The company’s strong competitive positioning in key technologies like LNG and ammonia should also benefit it further.

However, one key medium-term overhang is the anticipated completion of the Plaquemines LNG project. It is expected to wind down by early 2027. This creates a visibility gap, and investors are concerned about how the company will replace this revenue stream. Earlier expectations relied on the Lake Charles LNG project to ramp up and offset this decline. However, these hopes have been pushed out. The Lake Charles project has yet to secure final commercial backing. Energy Transfer (ET), the company executing this project, has shifted financial resources towards pipeline projects and is looking for third parties who may be interested in developing this project.

If we look at the stock price correction over the past few months, this Lake Charles project delay has been one of the main reasons behind its underperformance. However, global energy markets are shifting. Heightened geopolitical risks and supply disruptions may improve the economic rationale for large-scale LNG projects in the US. So, securing a third-party financial partner for Lake Charles looks more favorable than it did in prior periods. Should the project progress, it could help bridge the future revenue gap visibility.

The evolving geopolitical environment is also reinforcing the importance of energy security and independence. This remains a main driver for STS. While nearer-term disruptions from the Iran war may cause project delays or construction activity slowdown in the Middle East, the longer-term implications support capital investments in energy infrastructure, including new capacity additions as well as repairing facilities damaged by the current war. The company has a larger presence in the Middle East. There is no doubt that there are some short-term disruption risks due to war, but it also presents a long-term opportunity once stability returns, as the Middle East energy infrastructure remains crucial for global energy requirements.

Further, KBR is also shifting toward OpEx-oriented and recurring work, which provides an additional layer of support. Through organic initiatives and tuck-in acquisitions like SWAT, the company has been expanding its capabilities in areas such as asset utilization, digital solutions, and maintenance services within refinery, petrochemical, and renewable systems. This helps reduce KBR’s reliance on volatile, capital-intensive project cycles and should contribute to a much more stable revenue stream over time.

On the Mission Technology Solutions (MTS) side, revenue should see some headwinds in 1H26 as it laps tough comparisons and faces reduced EUCOM work. However, trends should improve as the year progresses. The company is well aligned with 2026 Defense Appropriations Act funding. Management anticipates bidding more than $25 billion in 2026, representing double-digit growth yr/yr. The company is also performing well internationally and has secured higher-value opportunities, including defense programs in Australia. Further, KBR’s positioning in digital engineering, space, and advanced defense capabilities is also strengthened by the recent LinQuest acquisition. This should support growth in higher-margin work in future years.

The evolving geopolitical situation, especially the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, could provide incremental growth for the MTS segment over the intermediate term. Usually, the periods of sustained military activity are followed by a heightened defense budget and rising demand for logistics, engineering, and support services.

Overall, while in the near term uncertainty persists around Lake Charles delays and Middle East project disruptions due to the Iran war, the broader demand backdrop remains positive. The structural tailwinds, such as energy security, a higher defense budget, and increasing demand for higher-value technology solutions, form a solid foundation for long-term growth. As these trends continue to play out, KBR looks well positioned to deliver good revenue growth in the long term.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

On margins, the company saw a 190 bps y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion in Q4 2025 despite revenue decline, benefiting from cost-saving initiatives, disciplined project execution, and favorable mix as low-margin EUCOM volumes declined. The MTS’ adjusted EBITDA margin was up 198 bps y/y. In the STS segment, the adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 101 bps y/y.

I am optimistic about the company’s long-term margin prospects. The ongoing global rollout of the Microsoft Dynamics ERP system is expected to enhance project visibility, improve cost management, and drive better efficiency gains. At the same time, the company is expanding its recurring, OPEX-related business. This should contribute to more predictable and resilient margin prospects compared to traditional capex-driven project work.

On the MTS side, a shift away from margin-dilutive work, coupled with a focus on higher-value, technology-led contracts, should drive margin in coming years. The ongoing wind-down of margin-dilutive EUCOM-related activity should provide an uplift to profitability as the portfolio mix improves. The company is also positioning itself toward differentiated opportunities, encompassing digital engineering, space, and advanced defense solutions. The integration of LinQuest should further strengthen this position. LinQuest increases KBR’s exposure to higher-margin national security and space-related programs. Growth in global markets such as the U.K. and Australia adds incremental support to margin upside, as they carry much more favorable margins compared to domestic government work.

Valuation

KBR currently trades at ~9.22x FY26 consensus EPS of $4.01 and 8.65x FY27 consensus EPS of $4.28. This is a significant discount to its ﬁve-year average forward P/E multiple of around 17.68x. On an EV/EBITDA basis also, the stock trades at roughly 7.1x forward EV/EBITDA compared to a historical average closer to 11.72x.

There are two primary drivers behind this valuation discount. First is a revenue visibility gap within the STS segment with the anticipated wind-down of the Plaquemines LNG project by early 2027. Final commercial backing has yet to be secured by the Lake Charles LNG project, which could have helped the company replace lost revenues. Second is the near-term uncertainty around construction activity and execution timelines in key regions given the current geopolitical environment. These factors are weighing heavily on investor sentiment and recent stock performance.

However, the longer-term fundamentals of the business appear to be strengthening. Both STS and MTS should benefit from structural drivers such as the growing emphasis on energy independence, rising global demand for LNG, and sustained defense spending. In fact, the economic case for large-scale energy infrastructure investments like Lake Charles has been reinforced by the ongoing Iran war and closure of the Straits of Hormuz. This war should also increase demand for defense and engineering services. The investment case is further strengthened by KBR’s focus on improving margin quality by increasing exposure to higher-margin, technology-driven, and recurring revenue streams. With a healthy backlog, an improving margin profile, and valuation at a significant discount versus historical levels, the risk-reward appears favorable.

The balance sheet is also strong, with 2.2x net debt to EBITDA that offers further upside from accretive M&As. I believe at the current levels, Plaquemines rolling off and Lake Charles not going forward, along with nearer-term disruption in the Middle East, are already getting priced in. The war, while unfortunate and a near-term headwind, should eventually benefit both its segments, with Mission Technology benefiting from defense-related spending and STS benefiting from energy independence and repair and rebuilding of energy infrastructure work. The Lake Charles LNG also has better chances of finding a financial backer post the recent Straits of Hormuz closure than before. Hence, I believe the stock can see some partial re-rating and narrow the gap with its historical valuation levels. So, I have a buy rating on it.

Risks

A prolonged war in the Middle East may continue to pressure projects there due to war-related disruption.

In addition to organic growth, the company also relies on bolt-on M&As. Inorganic growth carries risks related to overpaying for an acquisition, not realizing intended synergies, and other integration-related challenges.

Conclusion

While KBR faces some near-term headwinds from Middle East disruptions, the Plaquemines LNG project roll-off, and the Lake Charles project delay, they are already getting priced in with the stock trading at a 40% discount versus historical P/E. The increased emphasis on energy security and independence, healthy order trends in STS business, repair work related to energy infrastructure, and defense-related logistics work make the company's prospects attractive in the medium to long run. The company is also taking the right steps, like focusing on higher-margin work and increasing exposure to Opex-related work. This positions it well in the long run. I find risk-reward attractive and hence rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: FELG: A Price Correction Makes It An Attractive Growth ETF For Dip Buyers And Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF’s (FELG) price correction offers an attractive buying opportunity, as its valuations plunged to the multiyear low. Moreover, FELG’s portfolio of 112 large-cap growth stocks, with half of the concentration in tech stocks and exposure to the Magnificent 7 group, boosts its potential to deliver solid returns during a recovery phase and over the long term. Furthermore, the fund’s low per-share price makes it a worth considering ETF for retail investors. Its expense ratio of 0.18% is low, and liquidity is healthy. Therefore, I initiate coverage of FELG with a buy rating.

FELG Appears Cheap After a Price Correction

Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF currently trades around $36 per share, down 13% from its recent peak of $43 per share. Technically, securities are deemed to be in a correction phase after declining more than 10% from their 52-week high. A double-digit price decline along with a strong earnings outlook has helped in cooling large-cap growth valuations to the multiyear low. The category currently trades around 22x its forward earnings, down from its recent peak of around 30x. Its forward P/E is currently trading at its lowest level since late 2023.
